curriculum_source: CFA Program 2027 Level III, Volume 5 (Ethical and Professional Standards), Learning Module 5: Guidance for Standard IV: Duties to Employers

reading.reading_name: Guidance for Standard IV: Duties to Employers

drills.los_text: demonstrate a thorough knowledge of the CFA Institute Code of Ethics and Standards of Professional Conduct by interpreting the Code and Standards in various situations involving issues of professional integrity
drills.reading_name: Guidance for Standard IV: Duties to Employers

questions.stem: *Example 1 Magee*: manages pension accounts for Trust Assets; before resigning to join Fiduciary Management, asks 4 big accounts to leave Trust Assets and open accounts with Fiduciary; also persuades prospects (whom he previously presented to on behalf of Trust Assets) to sign with Fiduciary. *Most likely*:

options.A: Magee violated the employer–employee principle requiring him to act solely for his employer's benefit.
options.B: no violation.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Magee violated the employer–employee principle requiring him to act solely for his employer's benefit; Magee's duty is to Trust Assets as long as he is employed there; Magee's solicitation of Trust Assets' current clients and prospective clients while still employed by the firm is unethical and violates Standard IV(A).

Correct. Per Example 1 Comment: 'Magee violated the employer–employee principle requiring him to act solely for his employer's benefit. Magee's duty is to Trust Assets as long as he is employed there. Magee's solicitation of Trust Assets' current clients and prospective clients while still employed by the firm is unethical and violates Standard IV(A).'
rationales.B: Pre-departure solicitation violates.
rationales.C: Duty to current employer.

questions.stem: *Example 2 Hightower*: 15 years at Jason Investment; planning to leave and start own investment management business; plans to copy client list, account statements, marketing presentations, recommended list, computer models for asset allocation and stock selection, and his own spreadsheets. *Most likely*:

options.A: Hightower may copy the client lists and models because he personally developed or maintained them for fifteen years.
options.B: except with consent of their employer, departing members and candidates may NOT take employer property, which includes books, records, reports, and other materials, because taking such materials may interfere with employer's business opportunities.
options.C: Copying firm materials is acceptable if Hightower uses them only after his resignation becomes effective.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Books, records, reports, and similar materials created for the employer are employer property regardless of who built them.
rationales.B:
Full statement: except with consent of their employer, departing members and candidates may NOT take employer property, which includes books, records, reports, and other materials, because taking such materials may interfere with employer's business opportunities; taking ANY employer records, even those the member or candidate prepared, violates Standard IV(A); employer records include items stored in hard copy or any other medium (e.g., home computers, portable storage devices, cell phones).

Correct. Per Example 2 Comment: 'Except with the consent of their employer, departing members and candidates may not take employer property, which includes books, records, reports, and other materials, because taking such materials may interfere with their employer's business opportunities. Taking any employer records, even those the member or candidate prepared, violates Standard IV(A). Employer records include items stored in hard copy or any other medium (e.g., home computers, portable storage devices, cell phones).'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Taking employer property without consent is the violation; deferring its use does not cure the taking.

questions.stem: *Example 3 Madeline (unpaid intern)*: CFA candidate spends summer as unpaid intern at Murdoch; helps develop GIPS-compliance software; leaves for McMillan; copies the software before leaving because she believes it will help her at new job. *Most likely*:

options.A: There is no violation because Madeline was an unpaid intern and owed Murdoch no employee duties.
options.B: Madeline may copy the software because CFA candidates are not yet bound by the Standards.
options.C: even though Madeline does not receive monetary compensation for her services at Murdoch, she used firm resources in creating the software and is considered an employee because she receives compensation and benefits in the form of work experience and knowledge.

rationales.A: Incorrect. An unpaid intern using firm resources is treated as an employee; the software is firm property.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Candidates in the CFA Program are fully bound by the Code and Standards.
rationales.C:
Full statement: even though Madeline does not receive monetary compensation for her services at Murdoch, she used firm resources in creating the software and is considered an employee because she receives compensation and benefits in the form of work experience and knowledge; by copying the software, Madeline violated Standard IV(A) because she misappropriated Murdoch's property without permission.

Correct. Per Example 3 Comment: 'Even though Madeline does not receive monetary compensation for her services at Murdoch, she used firm resources in creating the software and is considered an employee because she receives compensation and benefits in the form of work experience and knowledge. By copying the software, Madeline violated Standard IV(A) because she misappropriated Murdoch's property without permission.'

questions.stem: *Example 4 Nash (disparaging employer)*: resigns after unsuccessful attempts to change churning practices; sends firm-authorized farewell notice to clients; when client asks about circumstances, Nash describes in detail the unethical practices, gives info about his new employer, and encourages the client to follow him. *Most likely*:

options.A: Nash violated Standard IV(A) by disparaging his current employer to his client and soliciting her business for his future employer.
options.B: There is no violation because Nash was protecting the client from his employer's churning practices.
options.C: Nash violated the Standards only by using a firm-authorized farewell notice for personal ends.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Nash violated Standard IV(A) by disparaging his current employer to his client and soliciting her business for his future employer; his actions are a violation EVEN IF the client would be better off at the new firm; after leaving the firm, Nash can inform his former clients of his concerns about their treatment and suggest they change investment managers.

Correct. Per Example 4 Comment: 'Nash violated Standard IV(A) by disparaging his current employer to his client and soliciting her business for his future employer. His actions are a violation even if the client would be better off at the new firm. After leaving the firm, Nash can inform his former clients of his concerns about their treatment and suggest they change investment managers.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Client-protection motives do not authorize disparaging the current employer and soliciting business for the future one while still employed.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The authorized notice was fine; the violation lies in the disparagement and solicitation that followed.

questions.stem: *Example 5 Chisolm (contacting former clients)*: Elliot hired Chisolm from competing firm; Chisolm wants to contact former clients; knows them well. *Most likely*:

options.A: Chisolm is permanently barred from contacting any client she served at her former firm.
options.B: because client records are the property of the firm, contacting former clients for any reason through the USE OF CLIENT LISTS OR OTHER INFORMATION TAKEN FROM A FORMER EMPLOYER WITHOUT PERMISSION is a violation of Standard IV(A).
options.C: Chisolm may reconstruct her former client list from memory, since memorized information is not a firm record.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Contact is not banned outright — it is the USE OF FIRM RECORDS (client lists and information taken from the former employer) that is prohibited; publicly available contact information may be used, subject to any agreements.
rationales.B:
Full statement: because client records are the property of the firm, contacting former clients for any reason through the USE OF CLIENT LISTS OR OTHER INFORMATION TAKEN FROM A FORMER EMPLOYER WITHOUT PERMISSION is a violation of Standard IV(A); however, simple knowledge of names and existence of former clients is NOT confidential information; skills or experience obtained while employed are not 'confidential' or 'privileged' information; in absence of an agreement that prohibits such conduct, as long as Chisolm maintains duty of loyalty before joining Elliot's firm, does not take steps to solicit clients until he has left former firm, and does not use material from his former employer without permission after he has left, he is NOT in violation.

Correct. Per Example 5 Comment: 'Because client records are the property of the firm, contacting former clients for any reason through the use of client lists or other information taken from a former employer without permission is a violation of Standard IV(A). Simple knowledge of the names and existence of former clients is not confidential information... In the absence of an agreement that prohibits such conduct, as long as Chisolm maintains his duty of loyalty to his employer before joining Elliot's firm, does not take steps to solicit clients until he has left his former firm, and does not use material from his former employer without its permission after he has left, he is not in violation of the Code and Standards.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Reconstructing the employer's client list — from memory or otherwise — is still using information belonging to the former firm.

questions.stem: *Example 6 Allen (preparation to leave)*: equity analyst; registers with government authorities to start competing investment company on her own time outside office; does not seek clients. *Most likely*:

options.A: Allen violated Standard IV(A) because registering a competing investment company with the regulatory authorities while still employed is inherently disloyal conduct, whatever her intentions or the timing of client outreach.
options.B: Allen must first disclose the planned registration to her employer and obtain its written consent, since regulatory filings of this kind are treated identically to rendering compensated outside services.
options.C: Allen's preparation for the new business by registering with the regulatory authorities does NOT conflict with the work for her employer IF the preparations have been done on Allen's own time outside the office AND if Allen will not be soliciting clients for the business or otherwise operating the new company until she has left her current employer.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Regulatory registration on her own time is PREPARATION, permissible when it does not impair her work or breach loyalty.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Preparation of this kind does not require consent; consent attaches to compensated outside services and competition while employed.
rationales.C: Correct. Per Example 6 Comment: 'Allen's preparation for the new business by registering with the regulatory authorities does not conflict with the work for her employer if the preparations have been done on Allen's own time outside the office and if Allen will not be soliciting clients for the business or otherwise operating the new company until she has left her current employer.'

questions.stem: *Example 7 (RFP response by soon-to-depart group)*: employees planning to depart in a few weeks; RFP sent to their current employer and all competitors; group believes new entity would be qualified to respond; submission period concludes before their resignations. *Most likely*:

options.A: a group of employees responding to an RFP that their employer is also responding to would lead to DIRECT COMPETITION between the employees and the employer.
options.B: There is no violation because the RFP was distributed to all competitors, making the process open.
options.C: The group needs no consent because responding to an RFP is preparation rather than competition.

rationales.A:
Full statement: a group of employees responding to an RFP that their employer is also responding to would lead to DIRECT COMPETITION between the employees and the employer; such conduct violates Standard IV(A) UNLESS the group of employees receives permission from their employer to respond to the RFP.

Correct. Per Example 7 Comment: 'A group of employees responding to an RFP that their employer is also responding to would lead to direct competition between the employees and the employer. Such conduct violates Standard IV(A) unless the group of employees receives permission from their employer to respond to the RFP.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Responding to the same RFP as one's current employer puts the group in DIRECT COMPETITION with the employer while still employed.
rationales.C: Incorrect. An actual bid against the employer is competition, not mere preparation — undertaking it requires the employer's consent.

questions.stem: *Example 8 Crome (private banker soliciting former clients)*: 8 years at YBSafe Bank; hired by competing bank with substantial signing bonus for advisers with own client portfolios; plans to solicit 70% of clients to follow. *Most likely*:

options.A: Crome may not contact any former YBSafe clients even after her employment terminates.
options.B: Crome may contact former clients upon termination of her employment with YBSafe Bank, but she is PROHIBITED from using client records built and kept with her in her capacity as an employee of YBSafe Bank.
options.C: The signing bonus obligates Crome to deliver her client portfolio, overriding YBSafe's property rights.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Post-termination contact is permitted; the prohibition is on using client records and information built and kept at YBSafe (public information and any non-compete terms aside).
rationales.B:
Full statement: Crome may contact former clients upon termination of her employment with YBSafe Bank, but she is PROHIBITED from using client records built and kept with her in her capacity as an employee of YBSafe Bank; client lists are PROPRIETARY INFORMATION of her former employer and must NOT be used for her or her new employer's benefit; use of written, electronic, or any other form of records from her prior employer, OTHER THAN PUBLICLY AVAILABLE INFORMATION, to contact her former clients at YBSafe Bank would be a violation of Standard IV(A).

Correct. Per Example 8 Comment: 'Crome may contact former clients upon termination of her employment with YBSafe Bank, but she is prohibited from using client records built and kept with her in her capacity as an employee of YBSafe Bank. Client lists are proprietary information of her former employer and must not be used for her or her new employer's benefit. The use of written, electronic, or any other form of records from her prior employer, other than publicly available information, to contact her former clients at YBSafe Bank would be a violation of Standard IV(A).'
rationales.C: Incorrect. No compensation arrangement with the new employer overrides the former employer's ownership of its records.

questions.stem: *Example 9 Webb (LinkedIn notification)*: terminated by RSI; contract has 2-year non-solicitation; while at RSI she connected with clients through LinkedIn network; updates LinkedIn profile several days after departure; LinkedIn auto-sends notification to entire network. *Most likely*:

options.A: violation — solicitation.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: Webb's actions did NOT violate Standard IV(A).

rationales.A: Post-departure profile update ≠ solicitation.
rationales.B: Post-departure notification OK.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Webb's actions did NOT violate Standard IV(A); Webb updated her LinkedIn profile only AFTER her employment ended; the updated employment profile notification by LinkedIn does NOT amount to solicitation of clients; best practice would dictate that Webb maintain separate accounts for her personal and professional social media activities; at a minimum, prior to her departure from the firm, Webb should discuss with RSI how to address any client information contained in her social media networks.

Correct. Per Example 9 Comment: 'Webb's actions did not violate Standard IV(A). Webb updated her LinkedIn profile only after her employment ended. The updated employment profile notification by LinkedIn does not amount to solicitation of clients. Best practice would dictate that Webb maintain separate accounts for her personal and professional social media activities. At a minimum, prior to her departure from the firm, Webb should discuss with RSI how to address any client information contained in her social media networks.'

questions.stem: *Example 10 Gupta (personal blog)*: research analyst at NIM (team-based process); NIM posts recommendations on password-protected website; Gupta uses his personal blog (widely available) to express his own opinions as counterpoint to NIM commentary. *Most likely*:

options.A: Gupta violated Standard IV(A) by disclosing confidential firm information through his personal blog.
options.B: no violation — different opinion.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Gupta violated Standard IV(A) by disclosing confidential firm information through his personal blog; the recommendations on the firm's blog to clients are NOT freely available across the internet, but his personal blog post INDIRECTLY provides the firm's recommendations; additionally, by posting research commentary on his personal blog, Gupta is using firm resources for his personal advantage.

Correct. Per Example 10 Comment: 'Gupta violated Standard IV(A) by disclosing confidential firm information through his personal blog. The recommendations on the firm's blog to clients are not freely available across the internet, but his personal blog post indirectly provides the firm's recommendations. Additionally, by posting research commentary on his personal blog, Gupta is using firm resources for his personal advantage.'
rationales.B: Indirect disclosure + firm resource use.
rationales.C: Confidential firm info.

questions.stem: *Independent business* under IV(A) *most likely*:

options.A: Standard IV(A) prohibits members from operating any independent business while employed, regardless of disclosure or consent.
options.B: although Standard IV(A) does NOT preclude members or candidates from engaging in an independent business while still employed, members and candidates are PROHIBITED from providing a service offered by their employer without their employer's consent because such conduct would conflict with employer's interests.
options.C: Independent business activity requires no employer involvement so long as it is conducted entirely outside working hours.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Independent business is not precluded; what is prohibited is rendering compensated services without consent from both employer and outside client.
rationales.B:
Full statement: although Standard IV(A) does NOT preclude members or candidates from engaging in an independent business while still employed, members and candidates are PROHIBITED from providing a service offered by their employer without their employer's consent because such conduct would conflict with employer's interests; members/candidates are NOT prohibited from PREPARING to enter into independent business so long as they do not solicit or provide services to clients or otherwise cause harm to employer; who plan to engage in independent business for compensation while employed must NOTIFY employer and describe types of services they will render to prospective independent clients, expected duration of services, and compensation for services; must NOT render services until they receive consent from employer to all terms of the arrangement.

Correct. Per Standard IV(A) Guidance on Independent Business: 'Although Standard IV(A) does not preclude members or candidates from engaging in an independent business while still employed, members and candidates are prohibited from providing a service offered by their employer without their employer's consent because such conduct would conflict with the interests of their employer. Members and candidates are not prohibited from preparing to enter into an independent business so long as they do not solicit or provide services to clients or otherwise cause harm to their employer. Members and candidates who plan to engage in an independent business for compensation while employed must notify their employer and describe the types of services they will render to prospective independent clients, the expected duration of the services, and the compensation for the services. Members and candidates must not render services until they receive consent from their employer to all of the terms of the arrangement.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. After-hours conduct still requires notification and consent when services compete with or could deprive the employer.

questions.stem: *Departing preparations vs solicitation* under IV(A) *most likely*:

options.A: Any arrangements to enter a competitive business made before resignation automatically violate the duty of loyalty.
options.B: Departing employees may solicit their employer's clients during the notice period, provided solicitation occurs off premises.
options.C: a departing employee is generally FREE to make arrangements or preparations to go into competitive business before terminating the relationship with his or her employer AS LONG AS such preparations do not breach the employee's duty of loyalty.

rationales.A: Incorrect. PREPARATION to compete (e.g., forming an entity, arranging office space) is generally permitted before termination; it is acts that breach loyalty that are barred.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Soliciting the employer's clients while still employed breaches the duty regardless of where it occurs.
rationales.C:
Full statement: a departing employee is generally FREE to make arrangements or preparations to go into competitive business before terminating the relationship with his or her employer AS LONG AS such preparations do not breach the employee's duty of loyalty; members and candidates who are contemplating seeking other employment must NOT contact existing clients or potential clients prior to leaving their employer to discuss a potential change in their employment status; after providing notice to employer of intent to resign, members and candidates MAY inform the clients with whom they work that they are leaving and going to a new firm but must NOT communicate information in a manner that could be seen as explicitly or implicitly soliciting clients or business for the new employer; for instance, while they may PROVIDE THE NAME of their new employer, must not provide their contact information at their new employer before employment ends without permission of current employer; also must NOT describe to clients the services available at the new firm or in other ways implicitly or explicitly promote their new employer to their current firm's clients without permission of current employer.

Correct. Per Standard IV(A) Guidance on Leaving an Employer: 'A departing employee is generally free to make arrangements or preparations to go into a competitive business before terminating the relationship with his or her employer as long as such preparations do not breach the employee's duty of loyalty. Members and candidates who are contemplating seeking other employment must not contact existing clients or potential clients prior to leaving their employer to discuss a potential change in their employment status. After providing notice to their employer of their intent to resign, members and candidates may inform the clients with whom they work that they are leaving and going to a new firm but must not communicate information in a manner that could be seen as explicitly or implicitly soliciting clients or business for the new employer. For instance, while they may provide the name of their new employer, members and candidates must not provide their contact information at their new employer before their employment ends without permission of their current employer.'

questions.stem: *Whistleblowing exception* under IV(A) *most likely*:

options.A: circumstances may arise (e.g., when an employer is engaged in illegal or unethical activity) in which members and candidates must act contrary to their employer's interests in order to comply with their duties to the market and clients.
options.B: Acting contrary to an employer's interests is never permitted under Standard IV(A), whatever the circumstances.
options.C: Whistleblowing is permissible whenever it advances the employee's own position, such as strengthening a wrongful-dismissal claim.

rationales.A:
Full statement: circumstances may arise (e.g., when an employer is engaged in illegal or unethical activity) in which members and candidates must act contrary to their employer's interests in order to comply with their duties to the market and clients; in such instances, certain activities that would normally violate a member's or candidate's duty to his or her employer (such as contradicting employer instructions, violating certain policies and procedures, or preserving a record by copying employer records) may be JUSTIFIED; such action would be permitted ONLY IF the intent is clearly aimed at protecting clients or the integrity of the market, NOT for personal gain.

Correct. Per Standard IV(A) Guidance on Whistle-Blowing: 'A member's or candidate's personal interests, as well as the interests of his or her employer, are secondary to protecting the integrity of capital markets and the interests of clients. Therefore, circumstances may arise (e.g., when an employer is engaged in illegal or unethical activity) in which members and candidates must act contrary to their employer's interests in order to comply with their duties to the market and clients. In such instances, certain activities that would normally violate a member's or candidate's duty to his or her employer (such as contradicting employer instructions, violating certain policies and procedures, or preserving a record by copying employer records) may be justified. Such action would be permitted only if the intent is clearly aimed at protecting clients or the integrity of the market, not for personal gain.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Where the employer engages in illegal or unethical activity, members may be obligated to act against its interests.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The exception exists to protect clients and market integrity — personal gain is not a qualifying motive.

questions.stem: *Post-employment use of skills and experience* under IV(A) *most likely*:

options.A: Skills and experience acquired during employment remain the employer's confidential property after departure.
options.B: once an employee has left the firm, the skills and experience that the employee obtained while employed are NOT 'confidential' or 'privileged' information.
options.C: A departing employee may use skills and experience only after a two-year cooling-off period.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Skills and experience — unlike client records and firm documents — are not confidential or privileged and travel with the employee.
rationales.B:
Full statement: once an employee has left the firm, the skills and experience that the employee obtained while employed are NOT 'confidential' or 'privileged' information; similarly, simple knowledge of the names and existence of former clients is generally NOT confidential information unless deemed as such by an agreement or by law; Standard IV(A) does NOT prohibit experience or knowledge gained at one employer from being used at another employer; work performed on behalf of employer, client lists, or other firm records — whether stored as paper copies or electronically on personal devices for member's/candidate's convenience — must be RETURNED to the employer or ERASED unless firm gives permission to keep those records after employment ends.

Correct. Per Standard IV(A) Guidance: 'Once an employee has left the firm, the skills and experience that the employee obtained while employed are not "confidential" or "privileged" information. Similarly, simple knowledge of the names and existence of former clients is generally not confidential information unless deemed as such by an agreement or by law. Standard IV(A) does not prohibit experience or knowledge gained at one employer from being used at another employer. Work performed on behalf of the employer, client lists, or other firm records — whether stored as paper copies or electronically on personal devices, such as phones, tablets, or laptop computers, for the member's or candidate's convenience — must be returned to the employer or erased unless the firm gives permission to keep those records after employment ends.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. No waiting period applies to using one's own skills and experience (contractual restrictions aside).

questions.stem: *Personal vs firm-approved social media accounts* under IV(A) *most likely*:

options.A: The Code draws no distinction between personal social media profiles and firm-approved accounts.
options.B: Members may not maintain any professional social media presence outside accounts owned by the employer.
options.C: members and candidates may create social media profiles solely for professional reasons, including firm-approved accounts for client engagements.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The distinction matters: firm-approved client-engagement accounts are firm assets, while purely personal profiles are not.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Members MAY create professional profiles of their own; the constraint concerns firm-approved accounts and firm policy compliance.
rationales.C:
Full statement: members and candidates may create social media profiles solely for professional reasons, including firm-approved accounts for client engagements; such firm-approved business-related accounts are PART OF THE FIRM'S ASSETS, thus requiring members and candidates to TRANSFER OR DELETE the accounts as directed by their firm's policies and procedures; best practice is to maintain SEPARATE accounts for personal and professional social media activities.

Correct. Per Standard IV(A) Guidance on Use of Social Media: 'Members and candidates may create social media profiles solely for professional reasons, including firm-approved accounts for client engagements. Such firm-approved business-related accounts are part of the firm's assets, thus requiring members and candidates to transfer or delete the accounts as directed by their firm's policies and procedures. Best practice for members and candidates is to maintain separate accounts for their personal and professional social media activities.'

questions.stem: *Practice Problem 1 independent practice* *most likely*:

options.A: Written consent from the employer is necessary to permit independent practice that could result in compensation or other benefits in competition with a member's or candidate's employer.
options.B: Prohibited from undertaking independent practice in competition.
options.C: Prohibited from making arrangements or preparations to go into a competitive business before terminating.

rationales.A: Correct. Per Practice Problem 1 Solution: 'Under Standard IV(A) Loyalty, members and candidates may undertake independent practice that may result in compensation or other benefit in competition with their employer as long as they obtain consent from their employer. C is not consistent with the Standards, because the Standards allow members and candidates to make arrangements or preparations to go into competitive business as long as those arrangements do not interfere with their duty to their current employer. A is not consistent with the Standards, because the Standards do not include a complete prohibition against undertaking independent practice.'
rationales.B: Not blanket prohibition.
rationales.C: Preparation is permitted.

questions.stem: *Practice Problem 2 Jackman ($10,000 adviser incentive)*: launches new firm; offers $10,000 incentive to any adviser who joins with min $200,000 in committed investments. *Most likely*:

options.A: Eligible if enough current clients switch and incentive is disclosed.
options.B: A member or candidate is not eligible for the new position unless he or she is currently unemployed because soliciting the clients of the member's or candidate's current employer is prohibited.
options.C: May not accept employment because incentive violates C&S.

rationales.A: Soliciting current employer's clients prohibited.
rationales.B:
Full statement: A member or candidate is not eligible for the new position unless he or she is currently unemployed because soliciting the clients of the member's or candidate's current employer is prohibited; Standard IV(A) prohibits soliciting current clients before leaving; there may be out-of-work members and candidates who can arrange the necessary commitments without violating the Code and Standards.

Correct. Per Practice Problem 2 Solution: 'Standard IV(A) Loyalty discusses activities permissible to members and candidates when they are leaving their current employer; soliciting clients is strictly prohibited. Thus, A is inconsistent with the Code and Standards, even with the required disclosure. B is incorrect because the offer does not directly violate the Code and Standards. There may be out-of-work members and candidates who can arrange the necessary commitments without violating the Code and Standards.'
rationales.C: Offer itself doesn't violate.

questions.stem: *Records or files after resignation* under IV(A) *most likely*:

options.A: After giving notice, an employee may take copies of work they personally produced, since authorship confers ownership.
options.B: Post-resignation handling of files is governed solely by local employment law, not the Code.
options.C: once notice is provided to the employer of the intent to resign, the member or candidate must follow the employer's policies and procedures related to notifying clients of his or her planned departure.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Work created on the employer's behalf is firm property; taking it requires the employer's permission regardless of authorship.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The member must follow the employer's notification and records policies as a Code obligation, not merely a legal one.
rationales.C:
Full statement: once notice is provided to the employer of the intent to resign, the member or candidate must follow the employer's policies and procedures related to notifying clients of his or her planned departure; in addition, the member or candidate must NOT take records or files to a new employer without the WRITTEN PERMISSION of the previous employer; members and candidates also must comply with their employer's policies regarding the use of social media during their employment, including the manner of disclosing their departure on firm social media platforms.

Correct. Per Standard IV(A) Guidance on Leaving an Employer: 'Once notice is provided to the employer of the intent to resign, the member or candidate must follow the employer's policies and procedures related to notifying clients of his or her planned departure. In addition, the member or candidate must not take records or files to a new employer without the written permission of the previous employer. Members and candidates also must comply with their employer's policies regarding the use of social media during their employment, including the manner of disclosing their departure on firm social media platforms.'

questions.stem: *When employment relationship ends* under IV(A) *most likely*:

options.A: when members and candidates are planning to leave their current employer, they must continue to act in the employer's best interest until the employment relationship ends.
options.B: The duty of loyalty ends the moment a letter of resignation is submitted.
options.C: The duty of loyalty ends when the employee accepts a written offer from the new firm.

rationales.A:
Full statement: when members and candidates are planning to leave their current employer, they must continue to act in the employer's best interest until the employment relationship ends; a letter of resignation does NOT necessarily signify the end of the relationship, especially if there is still a period of employment to complete; GENERALLY, the employment relationship ends once the employee is no longer being paid or no longer has responsibilities at the company; members and candidates must not engage in any activities that could conflict with their duty of loyalty to their employer until their employment relationship ends.

Correct. Per Standard IV(A) Guidance on Leaving an Employer: 'A letter of resignation does not necessarily signify the end of the relationship, especially if there is still a period of employment to complete. Generally, the employment relationship ends once the employee is no longer being paid or no longer has responsibilities at the company. Members and candidates must not engage in any activities that could conflict with their duty of loyalty to their employer until their employment relationship ends.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Loyalty continues until the employment relationship actually ends — generally when responsibilities and compensation cease — not at notice.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Accepting a future position does not terminate current duties; the member must act in the employer's interest until departure.

questions.stem: *Practice Problem 3 Jurgen (client offers extra compensation)*: portfolio manager; client offers to compensate her beyond firm compensation based on capital appreciation of his portfolio. *Most likely*:

options.A: Turn down — conflicts with other clients.
options.B: Obtain permission from her employer prior to accepting the compensation arrangement.
options.C: Turn down — undue short-term performance pressure.

rationales.A: Permission cures.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Obtain permission from her employer prior to accepting the compensation arrangement; the arrangement is not a violation of the Standards as long as Jurgen discloses the arrangement in writing to her employer and obtains permission from her employer prior to entering into the arrangement.

Correct. Per Practice Problem 3 Solution: 'This question involves Standard IV(B) Additional Compensation Arrangements. The arrangement described in the question — whereby Jurgen would be compensated beyond the compensation provided by her firm, on the basis of an account's performance — is not a violation of the Standards as long as Jurgen discloses the arrangement in writing to her employer and obtains permission from her employer prior to entering into the arrangement. A and B are incorrect; although the private compensation arrangement could conflict with the interests of other clients and lead to short-term performance pressures, members and candidates may enter into such agreements as long as they have disclosed the arrangements to their employer and obtained permission for the arrangement from their employer.'
rationales.C: Not automatic disqualifier.

questions.stem: *Example 2 Jones (nonmonetary board service)*: financial adviser Jones serves on board of Exercise Unlimited and recommends its stock; Jones does not disclose because no monetary compensation. *Most likely*:

options.A: There is no violation because Jones receives no monetary compensation for his board service.
options.B: Jones's only obligation is to disclose the board seat to Exercise Unlimited's shareholders.
options.C: Jones violated Standard IV(B) by failing to disclose to his employer benefits received in exchange for his services on the board of directors.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Nonmonetary benefits received for board service can create the same conflicts as cash and require employer disclosure under IV(B).
rationales.B: Incorrect. The disclosure IV(B) requires runs to Jones's EMPLOYER, whose consent protects against divided loyalties.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Jones violated Standard IV(B) by failing to disclose to his employer benefits received in exchange for his services on the board of directors; Jones' service as board director creates a conflict of interest because he has a personal incentive for recommending Exercise Unlimited stock; NONMONETARY compensation may create a conflict of interest in the same manner as being paid to serve as a director.

Correct. Per Example 2 Comment (Jones): 'Jones violated Standard IV(B) by failing to disclose to his employer benefits received in exchange for his services on the board of directors. Jones' service as a board director creates a conflict of interest because he has a personal incentive for recommending Exercise Unlimited stock. Nonmonetary compensation may create a conflict of interest in the same manner as being paid to serve as a director.'

questions.stem: *Example 3 Hollis (prior approval for ABC visit)*: gains appropriate approvals to accept ABC CEO meeting but declines offered travel; discloses dinner attended upon return. *Most likely*:

options.A: Hollis's actions did NOT violate Standard IV(B).
options.B: violation.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Hollis's actions did NOT violate Standard IV(B); through gaining approval before accepting the meeting and declining the offered travel arrangements, Hollis sought to avoid any potential conflicts of interest between his company and ABC Oil; by disclosing the dinner upon his return, Hollis enabled Specialty Investment Management to assess whether it has any impact on future reports and recommendations by Hollis related to ABC Oil.

Correct. Per Example 3 Comment (Hollis): 'Hollis's actions did not violate Standard IV(B). Through gaining approval before accepting the meeting and declining the offered travel arrangements, Hollis sought to avoid any potential conflicts of interest between his company and ABC Oil. By disclosing the dinner upon his return, Hollis enabled Specialty Investment Management to assess whether it has any impact on future reports and recommendations by Hollis related to ABC Oil.'
rationales.B: Pre-approval + declined travel + disclose = OK.
rationales.C: Prior approval + disclosure.

questions.stem: *Written consent definition* under IV(B) *most likely*:

options.A: Written consent means a physically signed paper document; electronic messages do not qualify.
options.B: 'written consent' includes any form of communication that can be documented (for example, communication via email that can be retrieved and documented).
options.C: Consent may be inferred from the employer's silence after the member sends notification.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Any documentable communication qualifies — a retrievable email satisfies the writing requirement.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Standard IV(B) Guidance: '"Written consent" includes any form of communication that can be documented (for example, communication via email that can be retrieved and documented).'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Consent must actually be given and documentable; silence is not consent.

questions.stem: *Purpose of IV(B) disclosure requirement* *most likely*:

options.A: The disclosure requirement exists primarily to let employers recapture outside income earned by employees.
options.B: IV(B) disclosure serves tax reporting purposes and has no bearing on professional conduct.
options.C: must obtain permission for additional compensation/benefits because such arrangements may affect loyalties and objectivity and create potential conflicts of interest.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The purpose is conflict assessment — such arrangements may affect loyalties and objectivity — not income recapture.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The rationale is protecting against divided loyalty and impaired objectivity, not tax administration.
rationales.C:
Full statement: must obtain permission for additional compensation/benefits because such arrangements may affect loyalties and objectivity and create potential conflicts of interest; disclosure allows employer to consider the outside arrangements when evaluating actions and motivations of members and candidates; moreover, the employer is entitled to have FULL KNOWLEDGE of all compensation/benefit arrangements so as to be able to assess the true cost of the services members or candidates are providing.

Correct. Per Standard IV(B) Guidance: 'Members and candidates must obtain permission for additional compensation/benefits because such arrangements may affect loyalties and objectivity and create potential conflicts of interest. Disclosure allows an employer to consider the outside arrangements when evaluating the actions and motivations of members and candidates. Moreover, the employer is entitled to have full knowledge of all compensation/benefit arrangements so as to be able to assess the true cost of the services members or candidates are providing.'

questions.stem: *Part-time / contract situations* under IV(B) *most likely*:

options.A: there may be instances in which a member or candidate is hired by an employer on a part-time or contract basis, allowing the member or candidate to work for multiple firms.
options.B: Part-time and contract workers fall outside Standard IV entirely, so no employer-duty rules apply to them.
options.C: Members working for multiple employers must give every employer identical duties and full-time loyalty simultaneously.

rationales.A:
Full statement: there may be instances in which a member or candidate is hired by an employer on a part-time or contract basis, allowing the member or candidate to work for multiple firms; during the contracting and hiring process, members and candidates should ADDRESS AND NEGOTIATE with their employer the parameters around their ability to provide services to other employers that may be competitive with their employer.

Correct. Per Standard IV(B) Guidance: 'There may be instances in which a member or candidate is hired by an employer on a part-time or contract basis, allowing the member or candidate to work for multiple firms. During the contracting and hiring process, members and candidates should address and negotiate with their employer the parameters around their ability to provide services to other employers that may be competitive with their employer.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The standard applies; multi-employer arrangements call for defining and agreeing duties (best negotiated up front) with each employer.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Duties owed to each employer are shaped by the terms of each engagement, agreed at the outset — not undifferentiated full-time loyalty to all.

questions.stem: *Example 1 Mattock (research supervision)*: senior VP responsible for compliance procedures for research dissemination; changes recommendation Timber Products from buy to sell; advises other H&V executives; Frampton (subordinate) immediately sells Timber for himself and clients; other personnel inform institutional customers before public dissemination. *Most likely*:

options.A: There is no violation because Mattock notified the affected parties before the recommendation change was published.
options.B: Mattock violated Standard IV(C) by failing to reasonably and adequately supervise the actions of those accountable to her.
options.C: Mattock's conduct implicates only Standard II(A), since advance notice of a recommendation change is inside information.

rationales.A: Incorrect. As the supervisor responsible for dissemination procedures, her failure to supervise those accountable to her is the IV(C) violation.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Mattock violated Standard IV(C) by failing to reasonably and adequately supervise the actions of those accountable to her; in her role, she must ensure that her firm has procedures for reviewing or recording any trading in the stock of a corporation that has been the subject of an unpublished change in recommendation; if adequate procedures had been established and followed, subordinates would have been informed of their duties, which would have facilitated detection of the improper sales by Frampton and prevented selected disclosure of the recommendation to clients.

Correct. Per Example 1 Comment (Mattock): 'Mattock violated Standard IV(C) by failing to reasonably and adequately supervise the actions of those accountable to her. In her role as senior vice president and head of research, she must ensure that her firm has procedures for reviewing or recording any trading in the stock of a corporation that has been the subject of an unpublished change in recommendation. If adequate procedures had been established and followed, subordinates would have been informed of their duties, which would have facilitated detection of the improper sales by Frampton and prevented selected disclosure of the recommendation to clients.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The tipping problem exists, but the question tested is her supervisory failure under IV(C).

questions.stem: *Example 2 Mason (supervising trading with self-interest)*: Wheeler VP responsible for compliance in trading department; part of her compensation based on commission revenues; notices trainee Edwards's suspicious trading (Highland — not on recommended list) but does nothing. *Most likely*:

options.A: There is no violation because the improper orders were entered by a trainee acting on his own initiative.
options.B: Mason's commission-based compensation excuses lighter supervision of revenue-producing trades.
options.C: Mason's failure to adequately review and investigate purchase orders in Highland stock executed by Edwards and her failure to supervise the trainee's activities violate Standard IV(C).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Supervisory duty covers subordinates' actions; a trainee's misconduct that adequate review would have caught is the supervisor's failure.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Self-interest in commission revenue aggravates rather than excuses the failure to review and investigate.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Mason's failure to adequately review and investigate purchase orders in Highland stock executed by Edwards and her failure to supervise the trainee's activities violate Standard IV(C); supervisors must be especially sensitive to actual or potential conflicts between their own self-interests and their supervisory responsibilities.

Correct. Per Example 2 Comment (Mason): 'Mason's failure to adequately review and investigate purchase orders in Highland stock executed by Edwards and her failure to supervise the trainee's activities violate Standard IV(C). Supervisors must be especially sensitive to actual or potential conflicts between their own self-interests and their supervisory responsibilities.'

questions.stem: *Example 3 Claudius (recordkeeping failure)*: assigns Tabbing to manage both fund and CEPSP portfolios; Tabbing places S&P 500 futures orders without designating account beforehand and assigns favorable executions to CEPSP after seeing market movement; Crozet has no written operating procedures or compliance manual for futures trading. *Most likely*:

options.A: Claudius violated Standard IV(C) by failing to adequately supervise Tabbing with respect to her S&P 500 trading.
options.B: only Tabbing violates.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Claudius violated Standard IV(C) by failing to adequately supervise Tabbing with respect to her S&P 500 trading; Claudius further violated Standard IV(C) by failing to establish recordkeeping and reporting procedures to prevent or detect Tabbing's violations; Claudius must make a reasonable effort to determine that adequate compliance procedures covering all employee trading activity are established, documented, communicated, and followed.

Correct. Per Example 3 Comment (Claudius): 'Claudius violated Standard IV(C) by failing to adequately supervise Tabbing with respect to her S&P 500 trading. Claudius further violated Standard IV(C) by failing to establish recordkeeping and reporting procedures to prevent or detect Tabbing's violations. Claudius must make a reasonable effort to determine that adequate compliance procedures covering all employee trading activity are established, documented, communicated, and followed.'
rationales.B: Supervisor violates by not establishing procedures.
rationales.C: Establish + document.

questions.stem: *Example 4 Rasmussen/Blair/Saunders*: trader observes hedge fund performance doesn't reflect market decline; approaches supervisor Blair (told not to ask questions), then compliance officer Saunders (told not to pursue). *Most likely*:

options.A: There is no violation because performance reporting accuracy is the fund administrator's responsibility.
options.B: under Standard IV(C), Blair and Saunders, the supervisor and the compliance officer, have the responsibility to review the concerns brought forth by Rasmussen.
options.C: Only Rasmussen had obligations here, and he discharged them by raising the issue once.

rationales.A: Incorrect. A supervisor and compliance officer presented with credible performance concerns must review them; dismissal is the IV(C) failure.
rationales.B:
Full statement: under Standard IV(C), Blair and Saunders, the supervisor and the compliance officer, have the responsibility to review the concerns brought forth by Rasmussen; supervisors have the responsibility of establishing and encouraging an ethical culture in the firm and investigating potential misconduct; the DISMISSAL of Rasmussen's question violates Standard IV(C) and undermines the firm's ethical operations.

Correct. Per Example 4 Comment (Rasmussen): 'Rasmussen has concerns about potential misconduct at her firm and brings them to the attention of supervisory personnel. Under Standard IV(C), Blair and Saunders, the supervisor and the compliance officer, have the responsibility to review the concerns brought forth by Rasmussen. Supervisors have the responsibility of establishing and encouraging an ethical culture in the firm and investigating potential misconduct. The dismissal of Rasmussen's question violates Standard IV(C) and undermines the firm's ethical operations.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Rasmussen acted properly, but the tested duty is Blair's and Saunders's obligation to investigate what was brought to them.

questions.stem: *Example 5 Graf (social media supervision)*: hires Burdette to expand social media presence; had not yet established policies for online communications; Burdette posts summary of buy recommendation for Sun Drive on personal accounts. *Most likely*:

options.A: only Burdette violates.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: Graf violated Standard IV(C) by failing to reasonably supervise Burdette with respect to her actions.

rationales.A: Failure to establish social-media policies.
rationales.B: Social media procedures needed.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Graf violated Standard IV(C) by failing to reasonably supervise Burdette with respect to her actions; he did not establish reasonable procedures to prevent the unauthorized dissemination of company research through social media networks; Graf must make sure all employees receive regular training about FIM's policies and procedures, including the appropriate business use of personal social media networks.

Correct. Per Example 5 Comment (Graf): 'Graf violated Standard IV(C) by failing to reasonably supervise Burdette with respect to her actions. He did not establish reasonable procedures to prevent the unauthorized dissemination of company research through social media networks. Graf must make sure all employees receive regular training about FIM's policies and procedures, including the appropriate business use of personal social media networks.'

questions.stem: *Example 6 Bartlett (branch employee supervision)*: FFWM compliance officer for all branches; Sokol recommends 100+ retail clients invest in complex Feeder Fund without adequately assessing suitability; results in $16M losses. *Most likely*:

options.A: Bartlett's actions violated Standard IV(C).
options.B: only Sokol violates.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Bartlett's actions violated Standard IV(C); Bartlett's inadequate policies, procedures, training, and supervision allowed Sokol to recommend the Feeder Fund without properly assessing whether the investment was suitable for each client; Bartlett failed to supervise Sokol and failed to adopt and implement written policies and procedures for FFWM designed to prevent investment advisory representatives at FFWM from recommending complex financial products to clients when they are not suitable.

Correct. Per Example 6 Comment (Bartlett): 'Bartlett's actions violated Standard IV(C). Bartlett's inadequate policies, procedures, training, and supervision allowed Sokol to recommend the Feeder Fund without properly assessing whether the investment was suitable for each client. Bartlett failed to supervise Sokol and failed to adopt and implement written policies and procedures for FFWM designed to prevent investment advisory representatives at FFWM from recommending complex financial products to clients when they are not suitable.'
rationales.B: Compliance officer violates too.
rationales.C: Branch supervision.

questions.stem: *Example 7 Bartolucci (detecting D'Addario violations)*: D'Addario provides artificially inflated price quotes for MBS to Amity Point in exchange for security trades; Bartolucci (CEO) knows D'Addario provides price quotes but does not develop policies or procedures. *Most likely*:

options.A: There is no violation because Bartolucci did not personally provide the inflated quotes.
options.B: Bartolucci's conduct violated Standard IV(C) by failing to develop, promote, and train employees on adequate policies governing the provision of price quotes to their customers.
options.C: Bartolucci's failing is one of record retention under Standard V(C), not supervision.

rationales.A: Incorrect. As CEO with knowledge of the arrangement, his failure to develop policies and train employees on price-quote conduct violates IV(C).
rationales.B:
Full statement: Bartolucci's conduct violated Standard IV(C) by failing to develop, promote, and train employees on adequate policies governing the provision of price quotes to their customers; Bartolucci failed to supervise D'Addario to prevent and detect D'Addario's illegal and unethical activities.

Correct. Per Example 7 Comment (Bartolucci): 'Bartolucci's conduct violated Standard IV(C) by failing to develop, promote, and train employees on adequate policies governing the provision of price quotes to their customers. Bartolucci failed to supervise D'Addarrio to prevent and detect D'Addario's illegal and unethical activities.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The defect is absent supervisory policies and training — squarely IV(C).

questions.stem: *Supervisory scope* under IV(C) *most likely*:

options.A: only CFA members.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: any investment professional who has employees subject to her or his control or influence.

rationales.A: All employees under control.
rationales.B: Any employee.
rationales.C:
Full statement: any investment professional who has employees subject to her or his control or influence — WHETHER OR NOT the employees are CFA Institute members, CFA charterholders, or candidates in the CFA Program — exercises supervisory responsibility.

Correct. Per Standard IV(C) Guidance: 'Any investment professional who has employees subject to her or his control or influence — whether or not the employees are CFA Institute members, CFA charterholders, or candidates in the CFA Program — exercises supervisory responsibility.'

questions.stem: *Delegation of supervisory duties* under IV(C) *most likely*:

options.A: members and candidates with oversight responsibilities for large numbers of employees may not be able to personally evaluate conduct of these employees on continuing basis.
options.B: Supervisory duties are personal and may never be delegated to subordinates.
options.C: Once supervisory duties are delegated, all responsibility transfers to the delegate.

rationales.A:
Full statement: members and candidates with oversight responsibilities for large numbers of employees may not be able to personally evaluate conduct of these employees on continuing basis; these members and candidates may DELEGATE supervisory duties to subordinates who directly oversee the other employees; a member's or candidate's responsibilities under Standard IV(C) include INSTRUCTING those subordinates to whom supervision is delegated about methods to promote compliance, including preventing and detecting violations of laws, rules, regulations, firm policies, and the Code and Standards.

Correct. Per Standard IV(C) Guidance: 'Members and candidates with oversight responsibilities for large numbers of employees may not be able to personally evaluate the conduct of these employees on a continuing basis. These members and candidates may delegate supervisory duties to subordinates who directly oversee the other employees. A member's or candidate's responsibilities under Standard IV(C) include instructing those subordinates to whom supervision is delegated about methods to promote compliance, including preventing and detecting violations of laws, rules, regulations, firm policies, and the Code and Standards.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Delegation is permitted — indeed necessary at scale — with proper instruction of the delegates.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Delegation does not relieve the supervisor of ultimate supervisory responsibility.

questions.stem: *Inadequate compliance system* under IV(C) *most likely*:

options.A: A member offered supervisory duties over an inadequate compliance system must simply accept and do their best.
options.B: a member or candidate with supervisory responsibility must bring an inadequate compliance system to the attention of the firm's senior managers and recommend corrective action.
options.C: The member should quietly compensate for the system's gaps through extra personal vigilance without alerting management.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The member must bring the inadequacy to senior management with recommended corrections — and consider declining supervisory responsibility in writing until it is fixed.
rationales.B:
Full statement: a member or candidate with supervisory responsibility must bring an inadequate compliance system to the attention of the firm's senior managers and recommend corrective action; if the member or candidate CLEARLY CANNOT DISCHARGE supervisory responsibilities because of absence of a compliance system or because of an inadequate compliance system, the member or candidate should DECLINE TO ACCEPT supervisory responsibility until the firm adopts reasonable procedures to allow adequate exercise of supervisory responsibility.

Correct. Per Standard IV(C) Guidance: 'A member or candidate with supervisory responsibility must bring an inadequate compliance system to the attention of the firm's senior managers and recommend corrective action. If the member or candidate clearly cannot discharge supervisory responsibilities because of the absence of a compliance system or because of an inadequate compliance system, the member or candidate should decline to accept supervisory responsibility until the firm adopts reasonable procedures to allow adequate exercise of supervisory responsibility.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Silent workarounds leave the defective system in place; escalation with recommendations is required.

questions.stem: *Response upon learning of violation* under IV(C) *most likely*:

options.A: A supervisor may accept the employee's assurance that the misconduct will not recur and take no further step.
options.B: The supervisor's obligation is limited to noting the incident in the employee's annual review.
options.C: once a supervisor learns that an employee has violated or may have violated the law or engaged in unethical behavior, the supervisor must PROMPTLY initiate an assessment to determine the extent of the wrongdoing.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Relying on assurances is inadequate; the supervisor must promptly initiate an assessment and limit further harm.
rationales.B: Incorrect. A performance-review notation is not the prompt investigation and interim control the standard requires.
rationales.C:
Full statement: once a supervisor learns that an employee has violated or may have violated the law or engaged in unethical behavior, the supervisor must PROMPTLY initiate an assessment to determine the extent of the wrongdoing; relying on an employee's statements about the extent of the violation or assurances that the wrongdoing will not reoccur is NOT enough; reporting the misconduct to appropriate compliance and management personnel and warning the employee to cease the activity are ALSO NOT ENOUGH; pending the outcome of the investigation, a supervisor must take steps to ensure that the violation will not be repeated, such as placing limits on the employee's activities or increasing the monitoring of the employee's activities.

Correct. Per Standard IV(C) Guidance: 'Once a supervisor learns that an employee has violated or may have violated the law or engaged in unethical behavior, the supervisor must promptly initiate an assessment to determine the extent of the wrongdoing. Relying on an employee's statements about the extent of the violation or assurances that the wrongdoing will not reoccur is not enough. Reporting the misconduct to the appropriate compliance and management personnel and warning the employee to cease the activity are also not enough. Pending the outcome of the investigation, a supervisor must take steps to ensure that the violation will not be repeated, such as placing limits on the employee's activities or increasing the monitoring of the employee's activities.'

questions.stem: *Detection responsibility* under IV(C) *most likely*:

options.A: members and candidates with supervisory responsibility must also make reasonable efforts to DETECT violations of laws, rules, regulations, and firm policies as well as unethical behavior.
options.B: Supervisors are responsible only for responding to violations reported to them, not for detection efforts.
options.C: Detection of violations is exclusively the internal audit function's mandate.

rationales.A:
Full statement: members and candidates with supervisory responsibility must also make reasonable efforts to DETECT violations of laws, rules, regulations, and firm policies as well as unethical behavior; supervisors exercise reasonable supervision by establishing and implementing written compliance procedures and ensuring those procedures are followed through periodic review; if a member or candidate has adopted reasonable procedures and taken steps to institute an effective compliance program, then he/she may NOT be in violation of Standard IV(C) if they do not detect violations that occur despite these efforts; fact that violations do occur may indicate that compliance procedures are inadequate; in addition, in some cases, merely enacting such procedures may not be sufficient to fulfill duty required by Standard IV(C); members and candidates may be in violation of Standard IV(C) if they know or should know that the procedures designed to promote compliance, including detecting and preventing violations, are not being followed.

Correct. Per Standard IV(C) Guidance on Supervision Includes Detection: 'Members and candidates with supervisory responsibility must also make reasonable efforts to detect violations of laws, rules, regulations, and firm policies as well as unethical behavior. Supervisors exercise reasonable supervision by establishing and implementing written compliance procedures and ensuring that those procedures are followed through periodic review. If a member or candidate has adopted reasonable procedures and taken steps to institute an effective compliance program, then the member or candidate may not be in violation of Standard IV(C) if he or she does not detect violations that occur despite these efforts. The fact that violations do occur may indicate, however, that the compliance procedures are inadequate.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Reasonable efforts to DETECT violations — through procedures and follow-through — are part of the supervisory duty.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Audit may assist, but the supervisory detection duty belongs to those with supervisory responsibility.

questions.stem: *Employer's Code of Ethics compliance* under IV(A) *most likely*:

options.A: Employer codes of ethics bind the firm as an entity but impose no obligations on individual members.
options.B: implicit in Standard IV(A) is the obligation of members and candidates to comply with the policies and procedures established by their employers that govern the employer–employee relationship.
options.C: Members must follow employer policies even when doing so would require violating the law or the Code.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Complying with the employer's governing policies and procedures is implicit in the member's IV(A) duty.
rationales.B:
Full statement: implicit in Standard IV(A) is the obligation of members and candidates to comply with the policies and procedures established by their employers that govern the employer–employee relationship — TO THE EXTENT THAT such policies and procedures do not conflict with applicable laws, rules, and regulations or the Code and Standards.

Correct. Per Standard IV(A) Guidance: 'Implicit in this standard is the obligation of members and candidates to comply with the policies and procedures established by their employers that govern the employer–employee relationship — to the extent that such policies and procedures do not conflict with applicable laws, rules, and regulations or the Code and Standards.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The obligation runs to lawful policies; illegal or Code-violating directives are never protected by loyalty.

questions.stem: *Personal obligations vs employer* under IV(A) *most likely*:

options.A: Standard IV(A) requires members to subordinate all personal interests to their employer's in every matter.
options.B: Loyalty obligations extend to the employer's interests in the member's private family and financial decisions.
options.C: this standard is NOT meant to be a blanket requirement to place employer interests ahead of personal interests in all matters.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The standard governs matters RELATED TO EMPLOYMENT; it is not a blanket subordination of personal life.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Personal matters unrelated to the employment relationship are outside the loyalty duty.
rationales.C:
Full statement: this standard is NOT meant to be a blanket requirement to place employer interests ahead of personal interests in all matters; the standard does not require members and candidates to subordinate important personal and family obligations to their work; members and candidates should enter into a dialogue with their employer about balancing personal and employment obligations when personal matters may interfere with their work on a regular or significant basis.

Correct. Per Standard IV(A) Guidance: 'This standard is not meant to be a blanket requirement to place employer interests ahead of personal interests in all matters. The standard does not require members and candidates to subordinate important personal and family obligations to their work. Members and candidates should enter into a dialogue with their employer about balancing personal and employment obligations when personal matters may interfere with their work on a regular or significant basis.'

questions.stem: *Nature of employment (contractor vs employee)* under IV(A) *most likely*:

options.A: Standard IV(A) applies in the employment context.
options.B: same rules.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Standard IV(A) applies in the employment context; a wide variety of business relationships exists in the investment industry; for instance, a member or candidate may be an employee or an independent contractor; members and candidates must DETERMINE whether they are employees or independent contractors in order to determine the applicability of Standard IV(A); duties in an independent contractor relationship are governed by the oral or written agreement between the member and the client; members and candidates should take care to clearly define the scope of their responsibilities and the expectations of each client in the context of each relationship; once a member or candidate establishes a relationship with a client, he or she has a duty to abide by the terms of the agreement.

Correct. Per Standard IV(A) Guidance on Nature of Employment: 'Standard IV(A) applies in the employment context. A wide variety of business relationships exists in the investment industry. For instance, a member or candidate may be an employee or an independent contractor. Members and candidates must determine whether they are employees or independent contractors in order to determine the applicability of Standard IV(A). A member's or candidate's duties in an independent contractor relationship are governed by the oral or written agreement between the member and the client.'
rationales.B: Determine status.
rationales.C: Contractor vs employee.

questions.stem: *Cannot promote new firm in name of protecting clients* under IV(A) *most likely*:

options.A: Members may promote a new firm's services before departure whenever they genuinely believe clients would benefit.
options.B: members and candidates CANNOT promote the services of a new firm in the name of protecting the interests of clients until their employment with their current firm ends.
options.C: Promotion of the new firm is acceptable if limited to clients who ask unprompted about the member's plans.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Client-benefit framing does not authorize pre-departure promotion; employment must first end.
rationales.B:
Full statement: members and candidates CANNOT promote the services of a new firm in the name of protecting the interests of clients until their employment with their current firm ends; members and candidates who believe the conduct or business practices of their employer are so egregious that they harm client interests are FREE TO RESIGN their position and SUBSEQUENTLY notify their former clients or other appropriate parties of their concerns.

Correct. Per Standard IV(A) Guidance on Leaving an Employer: 'Members and candidates cannot promote the services of a new firm in the name of protecting the interests of clients until their employment with their current firm ends. Members and candidates who believe the conduct or business practices of their employer are so egregious that they harm client interests are free to resign their position and subsequently notify their former clients or other appropriate parties of their concerns.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Even responsive conversations may state facts (such as the new employer's name) but not promote the new firm's services while employed.

questions.stem: *Activities that may violate IV(A) when leaving* *most likely* include:

options.A: no framework.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: unauthorized use of trade secrets.

rationales.A: Five violations.
rationales.B: Departure activities.
rationales.C:
Full statement: unauthorized use of trade secrets; misuse of confidential information, explicit or implicit solicitation of an employer's clients, or promotion of a new employer prior to cessation of employment; self-dealing (appropriating for one's own benefit property, a business opportunity, or information belonging to one's employer); unauthorized use of any firm property, including clients or client lists; and discussing a change in employment in a manner that disparages or denigrates the current employer such that it could cause harm to the firm's interests.

Correct. Per Standard IV(A) Guidance: 'Activities that may constitute a violation of Standard IV(A) include the following: unauthorized use of trade secrets; misuse of confidential information, explicit or implicit solicitation of an employer's clients, or promotion of a new employer prior to cessation of employment; self-dealing (appropriating for one's own benefit property, a business opportunity, or information belonging to one's employer); unauthorized use of any firm property, including clients or client lists; and discussing a change in employment in a manner that disparages or denigrates the current employer such that it could cause harm to the firm's interests.'

questions.stem: *Contact information at new firm before departure* under IV(A) *most likely*:

options.A: while members and candidates may provide the NAME of their new employer, they must NOT provide their contact information at their new employer before their employment ends without permission of their current employer.
options.B: Departing members may freely share their full new-firm contact details with clients during the notice period.
options.C: Members may not even reveal the name of their future employer until after their final day.

rationales.A:
Full statement: while members and candidates may provide the NAME of their new employer, they must NOT provide their contact information at their new employer before their employment ends without permission of their current employer; they also must not describe to clients the services available at the new firm or in other ways implicitly or explicitly promote their new employer to their current firm's clients without the permission of their current employer.

Correct. Per Standard IV(A) Guidance on Leaving an Employer: 'For instance, while they may provide the name of their new employer, members and candidates must not provide their contact information at their new employer before their employment ends without permission of their current employer. They also must not describe to clients the services available at the new firm or in other ways implicitly or explicitly promote their new employer to their current firm's clients without the permission of their current employer.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Providing new-firm CONTACT INFORMATION before departure crosses into solicitation; only the employer's name may be given.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Stating the new employer's NAME is permitted; it is contact details and solicitation that must wait.

questions.stem: *Adequate compliance procedures* — timing under IV(C) *most likely*:

options.A: post-incident.
options.B: to be effective, compliance procedures must be IN PLACE PRIOR to the occurrence of a legal or ethical violation.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Prior to violation.
rationales.B:
