Full statement: to be effective, compliance procedures must be IN PLACE PRIOR to the occurrence of a legal or ethical violation; although compliance procedures cannot be designed to anticipate every potential violation, they should be designed to anticipate the activities most likely to result in misconduct; compliance programs must be APPROPRIATE for the SIZE AND NATURE of the organization; the member or candidate should review model compliance procedures or other industry resources to ensure that the firm's procedures are adequate.

Correct. Per Standard IV(C) Guidance on System for Supervision: 'To be effective, compliance procedures must be in place prior to the occurrence of a legal or ethical violation. Although compliance procedures cannot be designed to anticipate every potential violation, they should be designed to anticipate the activities most likely to result in misconduct. Compliance programs must be appropriate for the size and nature of the organization. The member or candidate should review model compliance procedures or other industry resources to ensure that the firm's procedures are adequate.'
rationales.C: Prior + tailored.

questions.stem: *Effective compliance system elements* under IV(C) *most likely*:

options.A: code of ethics only.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: an effective compliance system goes BEYOND enacting a code of ethics.

rationales.A: Code + policies + review.
rationales.B: Beyond code.
rationales.C:
Full statement: an effective compliance system goes BEYOND enacting a code of ethics; it also includes establishing policies and procedures to achieve compliance with the code and applicable law and reviewing employee actions to determine whether they are following the rules.

Correct. Per Standard IV(C) Guidance: 'At a minimum, Standard IV(C) requires that members and candidates with supervisory responsibility make reasonable efforts to prevent and detect violations by ensuring the establishment of effective compliance systems. An effective compliance system goes beyond enacting a code of ethics; it also includes establishing policies and procedures to achieve compliance with the code and applicable law and reviewing employee actions to determine whether they are following the rules.'

drills.los_text: recommend practices and procedures designed to prevent violations of the Code and Standards
drills.reading_name: Guidance for Standard IV: Duties to Employers

questions.stem: *Understanding firm policies* under IV(A) *most likely*:

options.A: employers may establish codes of conduct and operating procedures for their employees to follow.
options.B: not required.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: employers may establish codes of conduct and operating procedures for their employees to follow; members and candidates should FULLY UNDERSTAND those policies and procedures to ensure that they are not in conflict with the Code and Standards; must understand any restrictions placed by the employer on offering similar services outside the firm while employed by the firm; the policy may outline procedures for requesting approval to undertake outside service or may be a strict prohibition of such service.

Correct. Per Standard IV(A) Compliance Practices: 'Employers may establish codes of conduct and operating procedures for their employees to follow. Members and candidates should fully understand those policies and procedures to ensure that they are not in conflict with the Code and Standards. Members and candidates must understand any restrictions placed by the employer on offering similar services outside the firm while employed by the firm.'
rationales.B: Understand employer policies.
rationales.C: Firm policies.

questions.stem: *Understand termination policies* under IV(A) *most likely*:

options.A: Termination policies concern only human resources staff, not the departing member.
options.B: members and candidates should CLEARLY UNDERSTAND the termination policies of their employer, including those that relate to the resignation process, how the termination will be disclosed to clients and staff, and whether updates posted through social media will be allowed.
options.C: Understanding termination policies is necessary only for members bound by non-compete agreements.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Members themselves should clearly understand resignation processes and how departures are handled.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Standard IV(A) Compliance Practices: 'Members and candidates should clearly understand the termination policies of their employer, including those that relate to the resignation process, how the termination will be disclosed to clients and staff, and whether updates posted through social media will be allowed.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The recommendation applies to all members, agreement-bound or not.

questions.stem: *Whistleblowing policies awareness* under IV(A) *most likely*:

options.A: Whistleblowing policies are relevant only to compliance officers, not ordinary members.
options.B: Members should discourage firms from formal whistleblowing programs, which invite frivolous reports.
options.C: members and candidates should be aware of their firm's policies related to WHISTLE-BLOWING and encourage their firms to adopt industry best practices in this area.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Members themselves should know their firm's whistleblowing policies.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The guidance is to ENCOURAGE adoption of industry best practices in this area.
rationales.C:
Full statement: members and candidates should be aware of their firm's policies related to WHISTLE-BLOWING and encourage their firms to adopt industry best practices in this area; many firms are required by regulatory mandates to establish confidential and anonymous reporting procedures that allow employees to report potentially unethical and illegal activities in the firm.

Correct. Per Standard IV(A) Compliance Practices: 'Members and candidates should be aware of their firm's policies related to whistle-blowing and encourage their firms to adopt industry best practices in this area. Many firms are required by regulatory mandates to establish confidential and anonymous reporting procedures that allow employees to report potentially unethical and illegal activities in the firm.'

questions.stem: *Providing employer with Code and Standards* under IV(A) *most likely*:

options.A: members and candidates are ENCOURAGED to provide their employers with a copy of the Code and Standards.
options.B: prohibited.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: members and candidates are ENCOURAGED to provide their employers with a copy of the Code and Standards; these materials will inform the employer of the responsibilities of a CFA Institute member or candidate in the CFA® Program; the Code and Standards also serve as a basis for questioning employer policies and practices that conflict with these responsibilities.

Correct. Per Standard IV(A) Guidance: 'Members and candidates are encouraged to provide their employers with a copy of the Code and Standards. These materials will inform the employer of the responsibilities of a CFA Institute member or candidate in the CFA® Program. The Code and Standards also serve as a basis for questioning employer policies and practices that conflict with these responsibilities.'
rationales.B: Provide copy — informs + basis for questioning.
rationales.C: Provide C&S.

questions.stem: *Employer's positive working environment* under IV(A) *most likely*:

options.A: employees only.
options.B: the employer–employee relationship imposes duties and responsibilities on BOTH PARTIES.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Both parties duties.
rationales.B:
Full statement: the employer–employee relationship imposes duties and responsibilities on BOTH PARTIES; employers must recognize the duties and responsibilities that they owe to their employees if they expect to have contented and productive employees; the employer is also responsible for a positive working environment, which includes an ETHICAL WORKPLACE.

Correct. Per Standard IV(A) Guidance: 'The employer–employee relationship imposes duties and responsibilities on both parties. Employers must recognize the duties and responsibilities that they owe to their employees if they expect to have contented and productive employees. The employer is also responsible for a positive working environment, which includes an ethical workplace.'
rationales.C: Mutual duties.

questions.stem: *Comply with employer social media policies* under IV(A) *most likely*:

options.A: Firm social media policies are advisory suggestions a member may override for client convenience.
options.B: Only marketing personnel must observe the firm's social media rules.
options.C: members and candidates must understand and abide by ALL applicable firm policies and regulations as to the acceptable use of social media to interact with clients and prospective clients.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Members MUST understand and abide by ALL applicable firm policies on social media use with clients.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The obligation attaches to every member interacting with clients through these channels.
rationales.C:
Full statement: members and candidates must understand and abide by ALL applicable firm policies and regulations as to the acceptable use of social media to interact with clients and prospective clients; this requirement is especially important when a member or candidate is planning to leave an employer; social media use makes determining how and when departure notification is delivered to clients more complex; communications through social media platforms that potentially reach current clients must adhere to the employer's policies and procedures regarding notification of departing employees.

Correct. Per Standard IV(A) Guidance on Use of Social Media: 'Members and candidates must understand and abide by all applicable firm policies and regulations as to the acceptable use of social media to interact with clients and prospective clients. This requirement is especially important when a member or candidate is planning to leave an employer. Social media use makes determining how and when departure notification is delivered to clients more complex... Communications through social media platforms that potentially reach current clients must adhere to the employer's policies and procedures regarding notification of departing employees.'

questions.stem: *Independent business notification requirements* under IV(A) *most likely*:

options.A: members and candidates who plan to engage in an independent business for compensation while employed must NOTIFY employer and describe (1) types of services they will render to prospective independent clients, (2) expected duration of services, and (3) compensation for the services.
options.B: Notification of independent business plans may be a simple heads-up without describing the services involved.
options.C: Notification is required only if the independent business will serve the employer's existing clients.

rationales.A:
Full statement: members and candidates who plan to engage in an independent business for compensation while employed must NOTIFY employer and describe (1) types of services they will render to prospective independent clients, (2) expected duration of services, and (3) compensation for the services; must NOT render services until they receive consent from employer to ALL of the terms of the arrangement.

Correct. Per Standard IV(A) Guidance on Independent Business: 'Members and candidates who plan to engage in an independent business for compensation while employed must notify their employer and describe the types of services they will render to prospective independent clients, the expected duration of the services, and the compensation for the services. Members and candidates must not render services until they receive consent from their employer to all of the terms of the arrangement.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The notification must DESCRIBE the arrangement — the types of services, expected duration, and compensation.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The duty attaches to compensated independent services generally, not only client-overlapping ones.

questions.stem: *Return/erase firm records upon departure* under IV(A) *most likely*:

options.A: keep for personal use.
options.B: work performed on behalf of the employer, client lists, or other firm records.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Return or erase.
rationales.B:
Full statement: work performed on behalf of the employer, client lists, or other firm records — whether stored as paper copies OR electronically on personal devices, such as phones, tablets, or laptop computers, for the member's or candidate's convenience — must be RETURNED to the employer or ERASED unless the firm gives permission to keep those records after employment ends.

Correct. Per Standard IV(A) Guidance: 'Work performed on behalf of the employer, client lists, or other firm records — whether stored as paper copies or electronically on personal devices, such as phones, tablets, or laptop computers, for the member's or candidate's convenience — must be returned to the employer or erased unless the firm gives permission to keep those records after employment ends.'
rationales.C: Return/erase records.

questions.stem: *Review non-solicitation and other post-employment agreements* under IV(A) *most likely*:

options.A: Post-employment agreements have no bearing on Code compliance and can be ignored by departing members.
options.B: Non-solicitation agreements are unenforceable against CFA charterholders as restraints on professional practice.
options.C: employers may require employees to sign agreements that PRECLUDE departing employees from engaging in certain conduct after they have left the firm.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Members should review and understand such agreements — breaching them can implicate both law and the duty of loyalty.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The charter confers no immunity from valid contractual restrictions.
rationales.C:
Full statement: employers may require employees to sign agreements that PRECLUDE departing employees from engaging in certain conduct after they have left the firm; members and candidates should TAKE CARE to review the terms of any such agreement when leaving their employer to determine what, if any, conduct those agreements may prohibit.

Correct. Per Standard IV(A) Guidance: 'However, employers may require employees to sign agreements that preclude departing employees from engaging in certain conduct after they have left the firm. Members and candidates should take care to review the terms of any such agreement when leaving their employer to determine what, if any, conduct those agreements may prohibit.'

questions.stem: *Maintain separate personal/professional social media accounts* under IV(A) best practice *most likely*:

options.A: best practice for members and candidates is to maintain SEPARATE accounts for their personal and professional social media activities.
options.B: Best practice is consolidating all personal and professional activity into a single account for transparency.
options.C: The Code discourages any professional use of social media by investment professionals.

rationales.A:
Full statement: best practice for members and candidates is to maintain SEPARATE accounts for their personal and professional social media activities; per Example 9 (Webb): 'Best practice would dictate that Webb maintain separate accounts for her personal and professional social media activities. At a minimum, prior to her departure from the firm, Webb should discuss with RSI how to address any client information contained in her social media networks.'

Correct. Per Standard IV(A) Guidance on Use of Social Media: 'Best practice for members and candidates is to maintain separate accounts for their personal and professional social media activities.' Also from Example 9 Comment: 'At a minimum, prior to her departure from the firm, Webb should discuss with RSI how to address any client information contained in her social media networks.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The recommended practice is the opposite: SEPARATE personal and professional accounts.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Professional social media use is accepted; the guidance concerns account separation and policy compliance.

questions.stem: *Compliance practice — disclose to whom under IV(B)* *most likely*:

options.A: Proposed outside compensation need only be disclosed if it exceeds the member's base salary.
options.B: members and candidates must disclose to their employer, through their supervisor or compliance officer, ANY compensation they propose to receive for services that is in addition to the compensation or benefits received from their employer, including performance incentives offered by clients.
options.C: Disclosure may be made to any colleague, since the duty is satisfied once someone at the firm knows.

rationales.A: Incorrect. ANY compensation for services expected to create a conflict must be disclosed — no materiality floor applies.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Standard IV(B) Compliance Practices: 'Members and candidates must disclose to their employer, through their supervisor or compliance officer, any compensation they propose to receive for services that is in addition to the compensation or benefits received from their employer, including performance incentives offered by clients.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Disclosure runs through the SUPERVISOR OR COMPLIANCE OFFICER, not any colleague.

questions.stem: *Contents of the IV(B) disclosure* *most likely*:

options.A: A bare statement that outside compensation exists satisfies the disclosure obligation.
options.B: The disclosure covers the arrangement's monetary amount only, not its nature or duration.
options.C: the disclosure should include the terms of any agreement under which a member or candidate will receive additional compensation, including (1) the NATURE of the compensation, (2) the APPROXIMATE AMOUNT of compensation, and (3) the DURATION of the agreement.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The disclosure should convey the TERMS of the agreement — nature, amount, and duration.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Nature and duration are required alongside amount so the employer can evaluate the conflict.
rationales.C: Correct. Per Standard IV(B) Compliance Practices: 'The disclosure should include the terms of any agreement under which a member or candidate will receive additional compensation, including the nature of the compensation, the approximate amount of compensation, and the duration of the agreement.'

questions.stem: *Third-party acknowledgment* under IV(B) *most likely*:

options.A: the party offering the additional compensation should ACKNOWLEDGE AND CONFIRM the details in the disclosure.
options.B: not required.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: the party offering the additional compensation should ACKNOWLEDGE AND CONFIRM the details in the disclosure; independent confirmation from the party offering compensation strengthens the disclosure and enables employer to verify terms.

Correct. Per Standard IV(B) Compliance Practices: 'The party offering the additional compensation should acknowledge and confirm the details in the disclosure.'
rationales.B: Third-party confirmation.
rationales.C: Confirmed disclosure.

questions.stem: *Board memberships/nonmonetary compensation disclosure* under IV(B) *most likely*:

options.A: Board service without cash compensation raises no conflict and needs no employer disclosure.
options.B: per Example 2 (Jones): nonmonetary compensation may create conflict of interest in same manner as being paid to serve as a director.
options.C: Board-related conflicts are addressed under Standard VI(A) alone and are outside IV(B)'s scope.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Nonmonetary benefits from directorships can conflict with employer interests exactly as cash would.
rationales.B:
Full statement: per Example 2 (Jones): nonmonetary compensation may create conflict of interest in same manner as being paid to serve as a director; members and candidates must disclose to their employer benefits received in exchange for such services — even where no cash is exchanged; obligation is triggered by any benefit that may compromise loyalty or objectivity.

Correct. Per Example 2 Comment (Jones): 'Jones violated Standard IV(B) by failing to disclose to his employer benefits received in exchange for his services on the board of directors. Jones' service as a board director creates a conflict of interest because he has a personal incentive for recommending Exercise Unlimited stock. Nonmonetary compensation may create a conflict of interest in the same manner as being paid to serve as a director.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. IV(B) reaches compensation and benefits from board service; VI(A) may apply in parallel, not instead.

questions.stem: *Preapproval for meetings/gifts and post-meeting disclosure* under IV(B) *most likely*:

options.A: Issuer-sponsored meetings and travel need no pre-approval so long as the member reports them afterward.
options.B: Members should decline all issuer meetings to eliminate any appearance of influence.
options.C: per Example 3 (Hollis): through gaining APPROVAL before accepting a meeting and DECLINING offered lavish travel arrangements, members and candidates can avoid potential conflicts of interest.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The illustrated practice is APPROVAL BEFORE the meeting, declining lavish arrangements, plus disclosure.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Meetings themselves are legitimate; the safeguards are pre-approval and rejecting lavish benefits.
rationales.C:
Full statement: per Example 3 (Hollis): through gaining APPROVAL before accepting a meeting and DECLINING offered lavish travel arrangements, members and candidates can avoid potential conflicts of interest; by disclosing any hospitality (dinner, etc.) upon return, member/candidate enables firm to assess whether it has any impact on future reports and recommendations.

Correct. Per Example 3 Comment (Hollis): 'Hollis's actions did not violate Standard IV(B). Through gaining approval before accepting the meeting and declining the offered travel arrangements, Hollis sought to avoid any potential conflicts of interest between his company and ABC Oil. By disclosing the dinner upon his return, Hollis enabled Specialty Investment Management to assess whether it has any impact on future reports and recommendations by Hollis related to ABC Oil.'

questions.stem: *Recommend codes of ethics* under IV(C) *most likely*:

options.A: members and candidates are ENCOURAGED to recommend that their employers adopt a code of ethics.
options.B: not needed.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: members and candidates are ENCOURAGED to recommend that their employers adopt a code of ethics; adoption of a code of ethics is critical to establishing a strong ethical foundation for investment advisory firms and their employees; codes of ethics formally emphasize and reinforce client loyalty responsibilities of investment firm personnel, protect investing clients by deterring misconduct, and protect the firm's reputation for integrity.

Correct. Per Standard IV(C) Compliance Practices — Codes of Ethics or Compliance Procedures: 'Members and candidates are encouraged to recommend that their employers adopt a code of ethics. Adoption of a code of ethics is critical to establishing a strong ethical foundation for investment advisory firms and their employees. Codes of ethics formally emphasize and reinforce the client loyalty responsibilities of investment firm personnel, protect investing clients by deterring misconduct, and protect the firm's reputation for integrity.'
rationales.B: Recommend adoption.
rationales.C: Code of ethics.

questions.stem: *Separation of code and procedures* under IV(C) *most likely*:

options.A: A firm's code of ethics should incorporate every detailed compliance procedure into a single unified document.
options.B: there is a DISTINCTION, however, between codes of ethics and the specific policies and procedures needed to ensure compliance with the codes and with securities laws and regulations.
options.C: Compliance procedures render a separate code of ethics unnecessary.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The guidance distinguishes principle-based codes from the detailed procedures that implement them — merging them buries principles in mechanics.
rationales.B:
Full statement: there is a DISTINCTION, however, between codes of ethics and the specific policies and procedures needed to ensure compliance with the codes and with securities laws and regulations; although both are important, codes of ethics should consist of FUNDAMENTAL, PRINCIPLE-BASED ETHICAL CONCEPTS that apply to all the firm's employees; in this way, firms can effectively convey to employees and clients the ethical ideals that investment professionals strive to achieve; supervisors implement these concepts through detailed, firm-wide compliance policies and procedures; standalone codes of ethics should be written in PLAIN LANGUAGE and should address general ethical concepts — should be unencumbered by numerous detailed procedures or boilerplate legal terminology.

Correct. Per Standard IV(C) Compliance Practices: 'There is a distinction, however, between codes of ethics and the specific policies and procedures needed to ensure compliance with the codes and with securities laws and regulations. Although both are important, codes of ethics should consist of fundamental, principle-based ethical concepts that apply to all the firm's employees... Supervisors implement these concepts through detailed, firm-wide compliance policies and procedures... Standalone codes of ethics should be written in plain language and should address general ethical concepts. They should be unencumbered by numerous detailed procedures or boilerplate legal terminology.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Both are needed: the code states the principles; procedures operationalize compliance.

questions.stem: *Providing code to clients* under IV(C) *most likely*:

options.A: internal only.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: members and candidates should encourage their employers to provide their codes of ethics to CLIENTS.

rationales.A: Share with clients.
rationales.B: Client visibility.
rationales.C:
Full statement: members and candidates should encourage their employers to provide their codes of ethics to CLIENTS; a simple, straightforward code of ethics, unencumbered by compliance procedures, will be effective in conveying that the firm is committed to conducting business in an ethical manner and in the best interests of the clients.

Correct. Per Standard IV(C) Compliance Practices: 'Members and candidates should encourage their employers to provide their codes of ethics to clients. A simple, straightforward code of ethics, unencumbered by compliance procedures, will be effective in conveying that the firm is committed to conducting business in an ethical manner and in the best interests of the clients.'

questions.stem: *Adequate compliance procedures characteristics* under IV(C) *most likely* include:

options.A: adequate compliance procedures should: be contained in clearly written and accessible resource that is TAILORED to firm's operations.
options.B: Compliance procedures are adequate whenever they replicate a template published by an industry association.
options.C: Adequacy requires procedures so comprehensive that they anticipate every possible violation.

rationales.A:
Full statement: adequate compliance procedures should: be contained in clearly written and accessible resource that is TAILORED to firm's operations; be drafted so procedures are easy to understand; designate a COMPLIANCE OFFICER whose authority and responsibility are clearly defined and who has the necessary resources and authority to implement firm's compliance procedures and investigate potential legal and ethical violations; describe the hierarchy of supervision and assign duties among supervisors; implement a system of checks and balances; describe the scope of the procedures; include procedures to document the monitoring and testing of compliance procedures; detail permissible conduct; and delineate procedures for reporting violations and sanctions.

Correct. Per Standard IV(C) Compliance Practices: 'Adequate compliance procedures should be contained in a clearly written and accessible resource that is tailored to the firm's operations, be drafted so that the procedures are easy to understand, designate a compliance officer whose authority and responsibility are clearly defined and who has the necessary resources and authority to implement the firm's compliance procedures and investigate potential legal and ethical violations, describe the hierarchy of supervision and assign duties among supervisors, implement a system of checks and balances, describe the scope of the procedures, include procedures to document the monitoring and testing of compliance procedures, detail permissible conduct, and delineate procedures for reporting violations and sanctions.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Adequate procedures must be TAILORED to the firm's own operations, not template copies.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The benchmark is a clearly written, accessible, tailored resource — not omniscient coverage.

questions.stem: *Actions once compliance program in place* under IV(C) *most likely*:

options.A: no framework.
options.B: once a compliance program is in place, a supervisor should: disseminate contents of program to appropriate personnel.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Post-program actions.
rationales.B:
Full statement: once a compliance program is in place, a supervisor should: disseminate contents of program to appropriate personnel; seek to periodically update the program to ensure that compliance measures are relevant, effective, and legally adequate; continually educate personnel regarding compliance procedures; issue periodic compliance reminders to appropriate personnel; incorporate a professional conduct evaluation as part of an employee's performance review; monitor and review actions of employees to ensure compliance and identify violators; and take necessary steps to enforce procedures once a violation has occurred.

Correct. Per Standard IV(C) Compliance Practices: 'Once a compliance program is in place, a supervisor should disseminate the contents of the program to appropriate personnel; seek to periodically update the program to ensure that the compliance measures are relevant, effective, and legally adequate; continually educate personnel regarding the compliance procedures; issue periodic compliance reminders to appropriate personnel; incorporate a professional conduct evaluation as part of an employee's performance review; monitor and review the actions of employees to ensure compliance and identify violators; and take the necessary steps to enforce the procedures once a violation has occurred.'
rationales.C: Post-program.

questions.stem: *Response after violation discovered* under IV(C) *most likely*:

options.A: no framework.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: once a violation is discovered, a supervisor should: RESPOND PROMPTLY.

rationales.A: Four-step response.
rationales.B: Response steps.
rationales.C:
Full statement: once a violation is discovered, a supervisor should: RESPOND PROMPTLY; ensure a thorough investigation of the activities is conducted to determine scope of wrongdoing; INCREASE SUPERVISION or place appropriate limitations on alleged offender pending outcome of investigation; and REVIEW PROCEDURES for potential changes necessary to prevent future violations from occurring.

Correct. Per Standard IV(C) Compliance Practices: 'Once a violation is discovered, a supervisor should respond promptly, ensure a thorough investigation of the activities is conducted to determine the scope of the wrongdoing, increase supervision or place appropriate limitations on the alleged offender pending the outcome of the investigation, and review procedures for potential changes necessary to prevent future violations from occurring.'

questions.stem: *Education and training programs* under IV(C) *most likely*:

options.A: regular ethics and compliance training, in conjunction with adoption of a code of ethics, is CRITICAL to employers seeking to establish a strong culture of integrity and to provide an environment in which employees routinely engage in ethical conduct and comply with the law.
options.B: Once a firm has formally adopted a written code of ethics, separate ethics and compliance training becomes redundant, because the code itself communicates the required standards to every employee.
options.C: A single comprehensive ethics session delivered at onboarding satisfies the training recommendation for the remainder of an employee's tenure, provided attendance is documented.

rationales.A:
Full statement: regular ethics and compliance training, in conjunction with adoption of a code of ethics, is CRITICAL to employers seeking to establish a strong culture of integrity and to provide an environment in which employees routinely engage in ethical conduct and comply with the law; training and education assist individuals in both recognizing areas that are prone to ethical and legal pitfalls and identifying those circumstances and influences that can impair ethical judgment; by implementing education programs, supervisors can train subordinates to put into practice what firm's code of ethics requires.

Correct. Per Standard IV(C) Compliance Practices — Implementation of Compliance Education and Training: 'Regular ethics and compliance training, in conjunction with adoption of a code of ethics, is critical to employers seeking to establish a strong culture of integrity and to provide an environment in which employees routinely engage in ethical conduct and comply with the law. Training and education assist individuals in both recognizing areas that are prone to ethical and legal pitfalls and identifying those circumstances and influences that can impair ethical judgment. By implementing education programs, supervisors can train their subordinates to put into practice what the firm's code of ethics requires.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Training works IN CONJUNCTION with the code; adoption alone does not build the culture.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The guidance calls for REGULAR, ongoing ethics and compliance training.

questions.stem: *Establish appropriate incentive structure* under IV(C) *most likely*:

options.A: Incentive review is limited to confirming that revenue targets are ambitious enough.
options.B: supervisors and firms must look CLOSELY at their incentive structure to determine whether the structure encourages profits and returns at the expense of ethically appropriate conduct.
options.C: Compensation design is a board prerogative outside the reach of supervisory analysis.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The review asks whether the structure rewards profits AT THE EXPENSE of ethically appropriate conduct.
rationales.B:
Full statement: supervisors and firms must look CLOSELY at their incentive structure to determine whether the structure encourages profits and returns at the expense of ethically appropriate conduct; problematic reward structures may not take into account how desired outcomes are achieved and encourage dysfunctional or counterproductive behavior; employees will work to achieve a culture of integrity when compensation and incentives are tied to HOW OUTCOMES ARE ACHIEVED rather than how much revenue is generated for the firm.

Correct. Per Standard IV(C) Compliance Practices — Establish an Appropriate Incentive Structure: 'Supervisors and firms must look closely at their incentive structure to determine whether the structure encourages profits and returns at the expense of ethically appropriate conduct. Problematic reward structures may not take into account how desired outcomes are achieved and encourage dysfunctional or counterproductive behavior. Employees will work to achieve a culture of integrity when compensation and incentives are tied to how outcomes are achieved rather than how much revenue is generated for the firm.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Supervisors and firms must examine how incentives shape conduct — it is squarely within IV(C) practice.

questions.stem: *Effective compliance tools recommended by IV(C)* *most likely* include:

options.A: A supervisor whose firm refuses adequate compliance resources has no further avenues and bears no responsibility.
options.B: Compliance tools are limited to electronic trade surveillance systems.
options.C: members and candidates with supervisory responsibility but limited authority may work with their own superiors and within the firm structure to develop and implement effective compliance tools, including but not limited to: (1) a code of ethics.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Even with limited authority, supervisors should work with their own superiors and within the firm structure to develop and implement effective compliance tools.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The toolkit is broad — codes, procedures, training, incentive review — not surveillance software alone.
rationales.C:
Full statement: members and candidates with supervisory responsibility but limited authority may work with their own superiors and within the firm structure to develop and implement effective compliance tools, including but not limited to: (1) a code of ethics; (2) compliance policies and procedures; (3) education and training programs; (4) an incentive structure that rewards ethical conduct; and (5) adoption of firm-wide best practice standards (e.g., the GIPS standards and the CFA Institute Asset Manager Code of Professional Conduct).

Correct. Per Standard IV(C) Guidance: 'Such limitations should not prevent members and candidates from working with their own superiors and within the firm structure to develop and implement effective compliance tools, including but not limited to: a code of ethics, compliance policies and procedures, education and training programs, an incentive structure that rewards ethical conduct, and adoption of firm-wide best practice standards (e.g., the GIPS standards and the CFA Institute Asset Manager Code of Professional Conduct).'

questions.stem: *Understanding what constitutes an adequate compliance system* under IV(C) *most likely*:

options.A: members and candidates with supervisory responsibility must UNDERSTAND what constitutes an adequate compliance system for their firms and make reasonable efforts to see that appropriate compliance procedures are established, documented, communicated to covered personnel, and followed.
options.B: Knowing the firm's compliance architecture is the compliance department's job, not the supervisor's.
options.C: Supervisors discharge their duty by assuming the existing system is adequate until a regulator says otherwise.

rationales.A:
Full statement: members and candidates with supervisory responsibility must UNDERSTAND what constitutes an adequate compliance system for their firms and make reasonable efforts to see that appropriate compliance procedures are established, documented, communicated to covered personnel, and followed; 'adequate' procedures are those designed to meet industry standards, regulatory requirements, requirements of Code and Standards, and circumstances of firm; once compliance procedures are established, the supervisor must also make reasonable efforts to ensure that procedures are monitored and enforced.

Correct. Per Standard IV(C) Guidance on System for Supervision: 'Members and candidates with supervisory responsibility must understand what constitutes an adequate compliance system for their firms and make reasonable efforts to see that appropriate compliance procedures are established, documented, communicated to covered personnel, and followed. "Adequate" procedures are those designed to meet industry standards, regulatory requirements, the requirements of the Code and Standards, and the circumstances of the firm. Once compliance procedures are established, the supervisor must also make reasonable efforts to ensure that the procedures are monitored and enforced.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Supervisors must themselves UNDERSTAND what an adequate system looks like and make reasonable efforts to see one established and maintained.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Passive reliance until regulatory correction is not reasonable effort.

questions.stem: *Instruction of subordinates when delegating* under IV(C) *most likely*:

options.A: After delegating, a supervisor should stand back entirely so the delegate can exercise independent judgment.
options.B: a member's or candidate's responsibilities under Standard IV(C) include INSTRUCTING those subordinates to whom supervision is delegated about METHODS TO PROMOTE COMPLIANCE, including preventing and detecting violations of laws, rules, regulations, firm policies, and the Code and Standards.
options.C: Delegates require no instruction if they have previously held supervisory roles elsewhere.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The supervisor must INSTRUCT delegates on methods to promote compliance and prevent violations, retaining ultimate responsibility.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Standard IV(C) Guidance: 'A member's or candidate's responsibilities under Standard IV(C) include instructing those subordinates to whom supervision is delegated about methods to promote compliance, including preventing and detecting violations of laws, rules, regulations, firm policies, and the Code and Standards.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Prior experience does not replace instruction on this firm's compliance methods and expectations.

questions.stem: *Assessment of extent of wrongdoing* under IV(C) *most likely*:

options.A: The wrongdoer's confirmation that the conduct has stopped completes the supervisor's inquiry.
options.B: Assessment of wrongdoing may wait until the next scheduled compliance audit.
options.C: once a supervisor learns that an employee has violated or may have violated the law or engaged in unethical behavior, the supervisor must PROMPTLY initiate an assessment to determine the extent of the wrongdoing.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Relying on the wrongdoer's assurance is inadequate; a prompt assessment of the extent of the wrongdoing is required.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The response must be PROMPT, with interim steps to limit further harm — not deferred to an audit cycle.
rationales.C:
Full statement: once a supervisor learns that an employee has violated or may have violated the law or engaged in unethical behavior, the supervisor must PROMPTLY initiate an assessment to determine the extent of the wrongdoing; relying on an employee's statements about the extent of the violation or assurances that the wrongdoing will not reoccur is NOT ENOUGH; reporting to compliance and warning the employee to cease are also NOT ENOUGH; pending outcome of the investigation, supervisor must take steps to ensure that violation will not be repeated, such as placing LIMITS on employee's activities or INCREASING MONITORING of the employee's activities.

Correct. Per Standard IV(C) Guidance on System for Supervision: 'Once a supervisor learns that an employee has violated or may have violated the law or engaged in unethical behavior, the supervisor must promptly initiate an assessment to determine the extent of the wrongdoing. Relying on an employee's statements about the extent of the violation or assurances that the wrongdoing will not reoccur is not enough. Reporting the misconduct to the appropriate compliance and management personnel and warning the employee to cease the activity are also not enough. Pending the outcome of the investigation, a supervisor must take steps to ensure that the violation will not be repeated, such as placing limits on the employee's activities or increasing the monitoring of the employee's activities.'

questions.stem: *Professional conduct evaluation in performance reviews* under IV(C) *most likely*:

options.A: supervisors should incorporate a PROFESSIONAL CONDUCT EVALUATION as part of an employee's performance review.
options.B: not recommended.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: supervisors should incorporate a PROFESSIONAL CONDUCT EVALUATION as part of an employee's performance review; this reinforces ethical conduct as a measurable performance factor rather than a peripheral concern.

Correct. Per Standard IV(C) Compliance Practices: 'incorporate a professional conduct evaluation as part of an employee's performance review.'
rationales.B: Include in perf review.
rationales.C: Ethical review integration.

questions.stem: *Culture built by senior management* under IV(C) *most likely*:

options.A: An ethical culture is created from the bottom up by the daily choices of individual employees, so senior management's tone and example play only an incidental role in whether misconduct takes root.
options.B: even if individuals want to make the right choices and follow an ethical course of conduct and are aware of the obstacles that impair ethical conduct, they can still be influenced to act improperly by a corporate culture that embraces a 'succeed at all costs' mentality, stresses results regardless of the methods used to achieve those results, and does not reward ethical behavior.
options.C: A sufficiently strong culture of integrity eliminates the need for formal compliance procedures, since employees who share the right values will not require written policies, monitoring, or enforcement mechanisms.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Individuals who want to act well can be undermined without a culture set and reinforced from the top.
rationales.B:
Full statement: even if individuals want to make the right choices and follow an ethical course of conduct and are aware of the obstacles that impair ethical conduct, they can still be influenced to act improperly by a corporate culture that embraces a 'succeed at all costs' mentality, stresses results regardless of the methods used to achieve those results, and does not reward ethical behavior; supervisors can reinforce an individual's natural desire to act ethically by building a culture of integrity in the workplace.

Correct. Per Standard IV(C) Guidance on Establish an Appropriate Incentive Structure: 'Even if individuals want to make the right choices and follow an ethical course of conduct and are aware of the obstacles that impair ethical conduct, they can still be influenced to act improperly by a corporate culture that embraces a "succeed at all costs" mentality, stresses results regardless of the methods used to achieve those results, and does not reward ethical behavior. Supervisors can reinforce an individual's natural desire to act ethically by building a culture of integrity in the workplace.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Culture and procedures are complements; neither substitutes for the other.

questions.stem: *Reasonable supervision scope* under IV(C) *most likely*:

options.A: Reasonable supervision is a fixed standard applied identically to every supervisor and firm.
options.B: Supervision adequacy is measured solely by the number of compliance staff employed.
options.C: the conduct that constitutes reasonable supervision in a particular case depends on the number of employees supervised and the work performed by those employees.

rationales.A: Incorrect. What is reasonable DEPENDS on the number of employees supervised and the nature of their work.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Headcount is not the test; scope and nature of supervised activity drive the analysis.
rationales.C:
Full statement: the conduct that constitutes reasonable supervision in a particular case depends on the number of employees supervised and the work performed by those employees; supervisor's obligations scale with organizational context — small teams may allow personal review of conduct, larger teams require delegated supervisory structures and systemic controls.

Correct. Per Standard IV(C) Guidance: 'The conduct that constitutes reasonable supervision in a particular case depends on the number of employees supervised and the work performed by those employees.'
